title: What is CodeRef
summary: CodeRef is a multi-agent software ecosystem — a set of AI agents, each owning a domain, that coordinate through files and standards rather than a central app. This site is the front door: arrive here, understand the system, branch into any part.

title: The orchestrator
summary: ASSISTANT is the ecosystem coordinator — the lead agent that drives the whole system. The role is narrower and sharper than "knows everything": it owns the coordination, not the domains' work.

title: The org chart
summary: The ecosystem has 63 registered domains (35 active), grouped by project. The shape is fractal — ASSISTANT coordinates the ecosystem the way a project coordinator drives its own project, one level down.

title: How to navigate
summary: This site is the front door to CodeRef — the place to arrive and orient. The docs are the map; the repo is the territory.

headings.text: The site grows with the ecosystem

title: What is CODEREF-CORE
summary: CODEREF-CORE is the standalone code-intelligence engine of the ecosystem — a scanner + retrieval core delivered as a CLI. It reads a codebase, produces the .coderef/ artifact set, and builds semantic search over the same code. Everything else consumes its artifacts rather than re-scanning.

title: Scanning and artifacts
summary: How a project gets its .coderef/ artifacts — bootstrap with coderef-init, generate with populate-coderef, read quickly with coderef-scan, repair with coderef-pipeline, and keep current with coderef-watch.

title: Semantic search (the rag family)
summary: The rag skills build a vector index over a project's code and answer natural-language questions against it — rag-index to build, rag-search to query, rag-status to inspect, and coderef-rag-server as the REST surface other tools call.

title: Contract checks and foundation docs
summary: Two things the .coderef/ artifacts make possible — catching frontend/backend API-contract drift with the scan-frontend-calls + validate-routes pair, and generating living foundation docs from the scanned data.

title: What a skill is
summary: A skill is a map and a guide, not a script — it tells an agent what a workflow is for, when to reach for it, and the contract to honor. The corpus holds 168 skills across 17 purpose categories, and the category list itself is schema-canonical.

title: Anatomy of a skill package
summary: Every skill is a directory at SKILLS/{CATEGORY}/{skill-name}/ with a standard shape — SKILL.md as the agent-facing contract, skill.json as the machine manifest, a runner when there is deterministic work, and support folders only when earned.

title: Finding and creating skills
summary: Two surfaces find a skill — the generated SKILL-INDEX table and the /which-skill ranked search. One surface creates them — /new-coderef-skill. The standing rule: never freehand a workflow a canonical skill already owns.

title: Canonical vs wrapper
summary: A skill's real logic lives in exactly one place — the canonical package in the ASSISTANT repo. What's installed into each CLI surface is a thin wrapper that points back at it. Validators enforce the split.

title: The daily session
summary: A multi-agent day runs inside a daily session — a dated directory every domain joins, where work moves by dispatch and idle agents stay responsive on a wake-poll loop. This is the skill-side of how the loop runs.

title: The session lifecycle
summary: A daily session is created once, joined independently by each domain, and swept closed at day's end. One subdirectory per domain means no file locking is needed.

title: The dispatch flow
summary: Dispatches are how work moves between domains — a sender files one, the receiver accepts and completes it through a state-machine runner, and the result is written back onto the same artifact.

title: The wake-poll loop
summary: Between tasks an agent is never "off" — it sleeps on a scheduled wake, polls its inbox, and always re-arms the next wake. A loop that forgets to re-arm dies silently.

title: How the day is coordinated
summary: ASSISTANT opens the session, every agent checks in and arms a wake-poll loop, work flows as dispatches (files in inboxes, polled — no server), blockers route back as andon pulls, and the day ends with a liveness-aware close. Coordination is a protocol made of files and a rhythm, not a runtime.

title: The registry-db model
summary: CodeRef tracks its work in a small SQLite database — the single source of truth. The JSON files under TRACKING/ that agents read every day are generated views of it. When a view and the DB disagree, the DB wins.

title: The lifecycle of tracked entities
summary: CodeRef tracks four entity kinds — stubs, workorders, dispatches, and tickets. They form a pipeline from "an idea worth remembering" to "work that shipped," plus a parallel lane for bugs.

headings.text: The shape of the whole flow

title: Views-vs-DB discipline
summary: Editing a generated JSON view by hand is editing a copy — the change vanishes on the next regeneration, or worse, desyncs the view from the DB. Every registry has a designated writer; you change state by running it.

title: The canonical registries
summary: The registries worth knowing by name — all under TRACKING/, the view-typed ones regenerated from the DB, never edited. One of them, agent-domains.json, is the source of truth for "who am I" across the whole multi-agent system.
